fuzzy_prd: We need a framework layer that gives a web controller all seven standard request handlers — listing a collection, showing one record, rendering a blank creation form, rendering an edit form, creating a record from submitted fields, updating an existing record, and deleting one — without hand-writing those handlers for every resource. Each handler should do the conventional thing: load the right data from the backing store, expose it under a predictable name, and either render a view or redirect with a flash message. On success, writes redirect to the record's own page; on failure, they re-render the form. The redirect on destroy should always go to the collection page, carrying success or failure feedback. We also need format negotiation: the default HTML behavior stays the same, but when a JavaScript representation is requested a successful write should render a view instead of redirecting. Unsupported formats must produce a clean, neutral error line — no stack traces. Configurable overrides are needed: teams should be able to change what name the loaded record is exposed under (think of it like how our auth module aliases the current user), choose a custom field to look records up by instead of the default identifier, and disable specific actions so the redirect logic falls back gracefully. Deliver a clean library plus a test adapter reading from stdin.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def index(options={}, &block); def show(options={}, &block); def create(options={}, &block); def update(options={}, &block); def destroy(options={}, &block)

trigger_keywords: what is the exact flash message format for create
what notice is shown after successfully creating
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: flash.notice=<ResourceName> was successfully created. (e.g. 'Article was successfully created.'). For update: '<ResourceName> was successfully updated.' For destroy success: '<ResourceName> was successfully destroyed.' For destroy failure: 'flash.alert=<ResourceName> could not be destroyed.'

trigger_keywords: where does destroy redirect on failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: destroy ALWAYS redirects to the collection URL (http://test.host/<resources>) regardless of success or failure. On success: flash.notice. On failure: flash.alert. Status is always 302.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for transcript lines
what order do the response lines appear in
line order in the response transcript
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fixed line order: store=... lines first, then assign=<name> (with size=<n> for collections), then status=<http-code>, then exactly one of redirect=<url> or body=<view text>, then any flash (flash.notice=... or flash.alert=...).

trigger_keywords: what is the redirect fallback when show is disabled
where does create redirect when the show action is not available
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the show action is in config.disabled_actions, a successful create redirects to the collection URL (http://test.host/articles) instead of the member URL. The flash notice is unaffected.
The exact output is: error=unsupported_format\n — no host-language exception type, no message, no stack. Just that single neutral line.

trigger_keywords: how are submitted attributes rendered in store lines
format of attrs in store transcript
how are attributes sorted in the transcript
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attributes are rendered as key=value pairs sorted alphabetically by key, comma-and-space separated, inside braces: e.g. attrs={title=Hello}. Empty attributes render as attrs={}.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: like how our auth module aliases the current user
context_pointers.actual_meaning: config.entity_name / config.collection_name overrides in the controller configuration — these change the assign= name in the transcript output.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please consult start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_1_list_collection.json.description: Issue a read request for the whole collection of a resource. The controller queries the backing data store for every record and exposes the result under the resource's plural name, then renders the collection view with a success status. The transcript reports the data-store query that was made, the name of the exposed collection together with how many records it holds, the HTTP status, and the rendered view body.

cases.expected_output:
store=list_all
assign=articles size=3
status=200
body=Index HTML

feature1_2_show_member.json.description: Issue a read request for a single member of a resource identified by its id. The controller looks the record up in the data store by that id, exposes it under the resource's singular name, and renders the member view with a success status. The transcript reports the lookup performed (with the id used), the exposed name, the status, and the rendered body.

cases.expected_output:
store=find id=42
assign=article
status=200
body=Show HTML

feature1_3_new_form.json.description: Issue a read request for a blank member used to back a creation form. The controller asks the data store to build a fresh, unsaved record (with no submitted attributes), exposes it under the resource's singular name, and renders the new-member view with a success status.

cases.expected_output:
store=build attrs={}
assign=article
status=200
body=New HTML

feature1_4_edit_form.json.description: Issue a read request for an existing member used to back an edit form. The controller looks the record up by id, exposes it under the singular name, and renders the edit-member view with a success status.

cases.expected_output:
store=find id=42
assign=article
status=200
body=Edit HTML

feature2_1_create_member.json.description: Submit attributes to create a new member. The controller builds a record from the submitted attributes and asks the data store to persist it. On successful persistence the response is a redirect to the canonical URL of the newly created member and a success flash notice naming the resource. When persistence fails (the record reports validation errors) the controller re-renders the new-member view with a success status and sets no flash.

cases.expected_output:
store=build attrs={title=Hello}
store=save ok=true
assign=article
status=302
redirect=http://test.host/articles/42
flash.notice=Article was successfully created.

store=build attrs={title=Hello}
store=save ok=false
assign=article
status=200
body=New HTML

feature2_2_update_member.json.description: Submit attributes to update an existing member identified by id. The controller looks the record up by id and asks it to apply the submitted attributes. On success the response redirects to the member's canonical URL with a success flash notice naming the resource. On failure (validation errors) the controller re-renders the edit-member view with a success status and no flash.

cases.expected_output:
store=find id=42
store=update attrs={title=New} ok=true
assign=article
status=302
redirect=http://test.host/articles/42
flash.notice=Article was successfully updated.

store=find id=42
store=update attrs={title=New} ok=false
assign=article
status=200
body=Edit HTML

feature2_3_destroy_member.json.description: Request deletion of an existing member identified by id. The controller looks the record up by id and asks it to delete itself, then redirects to the collection URL. On success the flash carries a notice naming the resource; on failure the flash carries an alert naming the resource. Either way the response is a redirect to the collection.

cases.expected_output:
store=find id=42
store=destroy ok=true
assign=article
status=302
redirect=http://test.host/articles
flash.notice=Article was successfully destroyed.

store=find id=42
store=destroy ok=false
assign=article
status=302
redirect=http://test.host/articles
flash.alert=Article could not be destroyed.

feature3_format_negotiation.json.description: Drive write actions while requesting a non-default representation, and request an unsupported representation. When a script representation is requested and supported, a successful create or update renders the action's script view with a success status (instead of redirecting) while still setting the success flash notice. When a representation that the controller does not support is requested, the request fails and the error is reported as a neutral category rather than leaking any runtime detail.

cases.expected_output:
store=build attrs={title=Hello}
store=save ok=true
assign=article
status=200
body=Create JS
flash.notice=Article was successfully created.

feature4_1_custom_names.json.description: Configure the controller to expose its records under custom singular and plural names instead of the names derived from the resource. The chosen names appear as the exposed-variable names in read responses, while routing and rendering are unaffected.

cases.expected_output:
store=list_all
assign=writings size=2
status=200
body=Index HTML

store=find id=9
assign=writer
status=200
body=Show HTML

feature4_2_custom_lookup.json.description: Configure the controller to look members up by a custom attribute instead of the default id-based lookup. Member-scoped read and write actions then resolve the record through that attribute, which is visible in the data-store interaction reported in the transcript.

cases.expected_output:
store=find_by slug=forty-two
assign=article
status=200
body=Show HTML

feature5_redirect_fallback.json.description: Show how the post-write redirect target is resolved. By default a successful create redirects to the canonical URL of the created member. When the member (show) action is disabled for the controller, there is no member URL to redirect to, so the controller falls back to redirecting to the collection URL instead. The flash notice is unaffected.

cases.expected_output:
store=build attrs={title=Hello}
store=save ok=true
assign=article
status=302
redirect=http://test.host/articles/42
flash.notice=Article was successfully created.

store=build attrs={title=Hello}
store=save ok=true
assign=article
status=302
redirect=http://test.host/articles
flash.notice=Article was successfully created.
